_readme:
Blog posts. To add one: copy an entry, give it a unique slug (used in the URL /post?slug=...), set date as YYYY-MM-DD, and write body as an HTML string (use

,

,

  • ; single-quote any HTML attributes). Newest first is automatic by date. Author defaults to Radhakrishnama Raju.

posts.title: Buying an open plot? Run this 7-point clear-title check first
posts.author: Radhakrishnama Raju
posts.excerpt: An open plot is only as good as its title. Before you pay a rupee of advance, walk through these seven checks that separate a clean investment from a costly dispute.
posts.body:
A plot in a fast-growing corridor can be one of the best decisions you make. It can also become years of litigation if the title is not clean. The difference is due diligence, and most of it can be done before you commit. Here is the checklist we run on every plot before we put it in front of a client.

1. Layout approval

Confirm the venture is approved by the right authority: HMDA in Hyderabad, DTCP for most layouts in Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu, and BMRDA or the local planning authority in and around Bengaluru. Ask for the approval number and the sanctioned layout plan, then verify the plot number matches the sanctioned drawing.

2. Title chain

Trace ownership back through the link documents. A clean chain shows every transfer, with no unexplained gaps. If a seller cannot produce the parent documents, treat it as a red flag until proven otherwise.

3. Encumbrance certificate

Pull a current Encumbrance Certificate (EC) covering at least the last 13 to 15 years. It reveals registered mortgages, liens or prior sales on the property. A clean EC is non-negotiable.

4. Land records match

The survey number on the sale deed must match the revenue records, the pahani or adangal in Telangana and AP. Names, extent and classification should all line up.

5. Prohibited-property check

Verify the land is not in the prohibited list under Section 22-A of the Registration Act, and not government, assigned or endowment land. Sub-registrar offices maintain this list; a quick check here saves a great deal of pain later.

6. Physical survey

Have the boundaries and extent physically verified against the FMB (Field Measurement Book) sketch. What is on paper and what is on the ground should be the same. Encroachment and boundary mismatches are common and easy to miss.

7. Guidance value and registration

Check the government guidance value for the area so you know the minimum on which stamp duty and registration will be calculated, and budget accordingly. This also tells you whether the quoted price is realistic.

The short version

Approval, clean title chain, clean EC, matching records, not prohibited, boundaries verified, guidance value known. Tick all seven and you are buying with confidence.

We complete every one of these checks before recommending a plot. If you would like a shortlist of title-verified plots in your budget, talk to us or browse verified plots.

posts.title: HMDA, DTCP, RERA: what these approvals actually mean for you
posts.author: Radhakrishnama Raju
posts.excerpt: Approval acronyms get thrown around in every listing. Here is what each one really certifies, and why an unapproved property is a risk no discount can justify.
posts.body:
Every second listing promises it is approved. Approved by whom, and for what, is the question that protects your money. Here is a plain-language guide to the approvals that matter across South India.

HMDA

The Hyderabad Metropolitan Development Authority sanctions layouts and building plans within the Hyderabad metropolitan region. An HMDA-approved layout has been checked for road widths, open space, drainage and land use. It is the gold standard for plots in and around Hyderabad.

DTCP

The Directorate of Town and Country Planning approves layouts outside the major metropolitan authorities, common across Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu. A DTCP layout number, verifiable with the department, tells you the venture is legally sanctioned for residential use.

Local authorities in Bengaluru

Around Bengaluru you will see BMRDA, BDA and BBMP approvals depending on the location. Each governs planning and building sanction for its jurisdiction. Always confirm which authority applies to the specific plot and that the approval is genuine.

RERA

The Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act applies to apartment and larger plotted projects. A RERA-registered project must publish its approvals, timelines and carpet-area details, and the developer is held to them. For an under-construction flat, RERA registration is your single most useful protection.

Why unapproved is not worth it

Unapproved property is usually cheaper for a reason. It can be difficult to get a home loan on, harder to resell, and exposed to regularisation costs or, in the worst case, demolition. A genuine discount never compensates for a title you cannot defend.

Every property we list is checked against the right approving authority. See our verified listings, or ask us to verify a property you are considering elsewhere.

posts.title: Carpet, built-up, super built-up: the area you pay for vs the area you use
posts.author: Radhakrishnama Raju
posts.excerpt: Two flats quoted at the same 'square feet' can give you very different living space. Understanding the three area terms, and what RERA changed, keeps you from overpaying.
posts.body:
When a listing says 1,600 square feet, which 1,600 does it mean? The answer decides how much usable home you actually get. Here are the three terms, from the space you live in to the space you are billed for.

Carpet area

The actual usable area within the walls of your flat, the space you could lay a carpet on. It excludes the thickness of external walls but includes internal partition walls. This is the number that matters most for daily living.

Built-up area

Carpet area plus the walls and features like balconies. Typically around 10 to 15 percent more than carpet area. It reflects the physical footprint of your unit.

Super built-up area

Built-up area plus your share of common spaces, lobbies, staircases, lift wells, corridors. This is often what pricing has traditionally been quoted on, which is why two flats with the same super built-up can feel very different inside.

What RERA changed

Under RERA, developers of registered projects must sell and disclose on the basis of carpet area. That single change made pricing far more honest, because you are comparing the space you will actually use, not a loaded figure. When you compare flats, always ask for the carpet area and compare like with like.

A simple rule

Judge value on carpet area. Judge comfort on the layout of that carpet area. A well-planned 1,200 carpet-square-foot home can live larger than a poorly planned 1,400.

We list carpet-area details clearly and walk you through the true usable space on every visit. Browse apartments or tell us what you are looking for.

posts.title: Home loans made simple: how your EMI and eligibility are decided
posts.author: Radhakrishnama Raju
posts.excerpt: Before you fall in love with a property, know what you can comfortably borrow and repay. A clear-eyed guide to EMI, loan-to-value and the factors that move your rate.
posts.body:
A home loan is the largest financial commitment most families make. Understanding how it is calculated puts you in control of the conversation with your bank, and helps you shop within your real budget.

How EMI works

Your Equated Monthly Instalment is calculated on three inputs: the loan amount, the interest rate, and the tenure. Early EMIs are mostly interest; over time more of each payment goes toward principal. A longer tenure lowers the monthly EMI but increases the total interest you pay, so it is always a trade-off.

Loan-to-value: how much you can borrow

Banks fund a percentage of the property value and expect you to bring the rest as a down payment. As a broad guide under RBI norms, lenders can fund a higher share on smaller loans and a lower share on larger ones. Plan for a down payment of roughly 15 to 25 percent, plus registration and stamp duty on top.

What decides your eligibility

  • Income and obligations: lenders look at your net income after existing EMIs. A common rule of thumb is that total EMIs should stay within 40 to 50 percent of income.
  • Credit score: a strong score improves both approval odds and the rate you are offered.
  • Age and tenure: loans can run up to around 30 years, but the tenure is capped by your retirement age.
  • Property and title: the bank lends against a clear, approved title, another reason approvals matter.

Estimate before you shortlist

Run the numbers first so you view only what you can comfortably carry. Our EMI calculator gives you the monthly instalment, total interest and total payable in seconds. Rates and eligibility vary by lender, so treat the result as a planning guide and confirm the final terms with your bank.

We help our buyers coordinate home-loan approvals as part of the deal. Get in touch and we will point you in the right direction.

posts.title: Vastu that actually matters when buying a home or plot
posts.author: Radhakrishnama Raju
posts.excerpt: You do not need to be an expert to make Vastu-sensible choices. A practical guide to the handful of factors most families in South India care about, and how to weigh them.
posts.body:
For many families across the Telugu and South Indian markets, Vastu is part of the decision, and homes that respect it tend to resell more easily. You do not need to master the subject to buy well. A few widely-followed principles cover most of what buyers ask us about.

Plot facing and shape

East and north-facing plots are the most sought after and generally command a premium, though a well-designed home can work on any facing. Regular, square or rectangular plots are preferred over irregular shapes. Corner plots and those with roads on favourable sides are valued.

Entrance

The main door is given the most weight. North, east and the north-east are traditionally considered auspicious directions for the entrance. This is often the first thing a Vastu-conscious buyer will ask about.

Room placement

  • Kitchen: the south-east is the classic preference.
  • Master bedroom: the south-west.
  • Pooja room: the north-east.
  • Water and open space: more open space and water features toward the north and east.

Keep it in perspective

Vastu is one input among many: location, title, connectivity, price and build quality still decide whether a property is a good buy. Treat Vastu as a filter that narrows your shortlist, not the only lens. A minor deviation can often be balanced through design; a bad title cannot be balanced by anything.

Tell us your Vastu preferences and we will factor them into your shortlist from the start. Share what you are looking for.

posts.title: Your first home, step by step: from budget to registration
posts.author: Radhakrishnama Raju
posts.excerpt: Buying your first property feels overwhelming because no one lays out the whole path. Here is the entire journey in seven clear steps, so you always know what comes next.
posts.body:
The hardest part of buying your first home is not any single step, it is not knowing the sequence. Here is the full path, start to finish, so you can move with confidence.

1. Fix your real budget

Start with what you can comfortably repay, not the maximum a bank will lend. Factor in the down payment, registration and stamp duty, and a buffer. Our EMI calculator helps you set this honestly.

2. Get a loan pre-assessment

Knowing your eligibility upfront tells you your true ceiling and makes you a serious buyer when you negotiate.

3. Shortlist with the right filters

Location, connectivity, approvals, facing and future growth. Narrow to a handful that fit your budget and your priorities rather than chasing every option.

4. Visit, and visit properly

See the property at different times of day where you can. Check water, power backup, road access, and the neighbourhood, not just the flat or plot itself.

5. Due diligence on title

Approvals, title chain, encumbrance certificate, and for apartments, RERA registration. This is where a good advisor earns their keep. Never skip it to move faster.

6. Negotiate and agree terms

Price, payment schedule, what is included, and timelines in writing. Clarity here prevents disputes later.

7. Register

Pay stamp duty and registration, execute the sale deed at the sub-registrar office, and collect your registered documents and updated records. The keys, and the peace of mind, follow.

You do not have to do it alone

A first purchase is a big step, and the right guidance turns it from stressful to straightforward. We take first-time buyers through every one of these steps, with one accountable point of contact from shortlist to registration. Start a conversation whenever you are ready.
